steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Grasby et al. want to know whether perceived category similarity predicts purchase likelihood across extended-brand pairs. They already have the change-in-probability data for each pair. To test this, they need a second variable: a measure of perceived category similarity for those same pairs, so the two can be correlated.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Have a representative sample of the households rate the similarity of the product categories in each extended-brand pair, then determine how, if at all, those ratings correlate with the change in probability that the team calculated for each pair.: This directly supplies perceived category similarity ratings for each pair and tests whether they correlate with the team's probability data — both variables the research question requires. ✓
  • Choice B — Poll a representative sample of the households to determine the degree of brand recognition for each brand in the extended-brand pairs, then determine how, if at all, the degree of brand recognition correlates with the frequency with which a different group of households purchased at least one product of that brand.: Measures brand recognition, not category similarity, and correlates it against a different group's purchase frequency — neither variable matches the research question. ✗
  • Choice C — Poll a representative sample of the households to determine the degree of brand recognition of each brand in the extended-brand pairs, then determine how, if at all, the degree of brand recognition correlates with the average cost of each product in the pairs.: Also measures brand recognition (not category similarity) and correlates it with product cost — entirely off-topic for the research question. ✗
  • Choice D — Have a representative sample of the households rate the similarity of one product in each extended-brand pair to other products in the same category, then determine how, if at all, those ratings correlate with the change in probability that the team calculated for each pair.: Asks about within-category similarity (how similar a wristwatch is to other watches), not between-category similarity (how similar watches are to neckties) — the wrong comparison for a study of brand extensions across product categories. ✗
That matches Choice A — Have a representative sample of the households rate the similarity of the product categories in each extended-brand pair, then determine how, if at all, those ratings correlate with the change in probability that the team calculated for each pair..
